paper_title: OpenBioRQ: Unsolved Biomedical Research Questions for Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: A working citation looks like proof -- but the fact that a link resolves does not mean the cited paper supports the claim. I find that current agentic models rarely fabricate citations (over $99\%$ resolve), yet roughly $15.9\%$ link to the wrong paper. Existing benchmarks miss this failure mode: when a question has a fixed answer key, a model can reproduce the expected source from that key rather than independently verifying that the source supports the claim. I introduce \textbf{\openbiorq{}}, a retrieval-grounded agentic benchmark of $12{,}553$ unsolved biomedical research questions across $12$ domains that treats open questions as a faithfulness-and-abstention probe. To my knowledge, this is the first biomedical benchmark to combine an agentic setting -- where the model must issue multiple tool calls -- with unsolved questions that have no answer key. Openness is verified against real follow-up evidence rather than a model's parametric knowledge. Difficulty is empirical: I anchor it on questions that three open-weight reference models fail to answer, rather than on subjective hardness labels. On this hardest subset, held-out models from the same lineage as the difficulty anchors solve only ~17%, while three independent frontier agents (Gemini-3-Pro, Opus-4.7, GPT-5.5) span a wide 29-60% range. The benchmark is thus hard, non-saturating (the best agent still leaves ~33-40\% unsolved), and discriminating across capability tiers. Beyond difficulty, I observe agentic collapse on the hardest questions, where agents stop using their tools. For the most collapse-prone model, blocking tool access entirely barely changes its score -- so tools stop paying off exactly where they are needed most. A frozen per-question checklist raises inter-judge agreement from Spearman 0.35 to 0.82.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: When an agentic model answers biomedical question and attaches PMID, the resolvable identifier reads as evidence: reader follows the link, the record exists, and the claim inherits the authority of the literature. For example, asked about the long-term safety of mRNA vaccines, model reports that vaccine efficacy remained 91% against symptomatic COVID-19 and cites PMID 34407296. The identifier resolves to real, indexed articlebut an ophthalmology study that never mentions vaccine. The citation exists, Copyright 2026, Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved. Figure 1: Where OpenBioRQ sits among biomedical benchmarks: answer status (known genuinely open) against tool use (static agentic). Prior benchmarks closed-form, long-form, and agentic-with-answers (e.g., BioMysteryBench)all assume known answer. OpenBioRQ alone targets unsolved questions, in the shaded agentic unsolved quadrant, where wrong-paper citation, agentic collapse, and abstain-vs-confabulate become measurable. yet it does not support the claim. The failure is purely one of linking the claim to its source: the reported efficacy is roughly correct and the model even names the right trial in proseonly the emitted identifier points elsewhere. The example is verbatim trace from my audit, not hypothetical. Closed-form question answering (Tsatsaronis et al. 2015; Jin et al. 2019; Jin et al. 2021; Pal, Umapathi, and Sankarasubbu 2022) requires models to answer solvable questions, where the correct answerand often its supporting source is fixed in advance. Long-form question answering (Abacha et al. 2017; Abacha et al. 2019; Singhal et al. 2023a; Manes et al. 2024; Jeong et al. 2024) relaxes the answer format to free text, but still scores each response against fixed gold answer and its curated reference, so faithfulness is judged by overlap with pre-specified source rather than by whether the models own citations actually support its claims. Recently, agentic evaluations such as GeneGPT (Jin et al. 2024) and BioMysteryBench (Anthropic 2026) let models call external tools and gather evidence on their own, yet they still target questions with verifiable ground-truth answer, leaving unchecked whether each retrieved paper actually supports the claim it is attached to. These three families differ in format but share single assumption, known answer, and so occupy three corners of an answer-status agentic plane (Figure 1). The fourth corner is underexplored, and the reason to target it is structural, not aesthetic. The failures studiedwrong-paper citation, agentic collapse, and the choice between abstaining and confabulatingsurface only when the answer key is removed. When the answer is fixed in advance, model can pass by echoing the source it was handed, and these failures have no room to appear. Thus, OpenBioRQ targets: 12,553 unsolved biomedical research questions across 12 domains. pose them not to be answered against key, but to be attemptedand score how an agent behaves in the attempt. To my knowledge, it is the first benchmark in the agentic unsolved regime. Reframing the task this way turns evaluation into faithfulness-and-abstention probe. audit every citation an agent emits at two levels: (1) existence, whether the identifier resolves to real paper; (2) content support, whether that paper actually supports the claim. One might expect these to rise and fall together. In biomedical research, they come apart and are opposite to what other fields report. ResearchMath (Son et al. 2026) finds almost 54% of references in mathematical writing to be outright fabricated; biomedical agents rarely fabricate, with only 0.7% of 4,863 emitted citations failing to resolve. Of the citations that do resolve, 15.9% are wrong-paper that does not support the claim it is attached to (an LLM-judge estimate; 10.6% under different-family judge, 5). Because wrong-paper citation resolves, reader is more inclined to trust it; it may therefore be more hazardous than an obvious fabricationa severity gap pose as hypothesis. Scoring unsolved questions invites an immediate objection: with no key, how do we know question is genuinely open, and that it is genuinely hard? Three design choices answer this rather than assume it. (1) Framing question as open is not enoughdoing so alone collapses into confirmation bias. Instead, retrieval-grounded verifier re-judges each question against real follow-up evidence, and re-audit of the 657-question core finds none of them resolved; (2) the core retains only questions the open-weight reference roster jointly fails, and on this subset held-out models of the same lineage solve just 17% while three independent-lineage frontier agents span 2960%hard at the open-weight tier, yet non-saturating and discriminating, since even the strongest agent leaves over third unsolved; (3) answers are produced through multi-round tool use over ten biomedical REST APIs and graded against frozen per-question checklist, which raises inter-judge agreement from Spearman 0.35 to 0.82 (6). Beyond citations, OpenBioRQ surfaces an agentic collapse: on the hardest questions, agents abandon their tools, and for the most collapse-prone model, blocking tool use altogether barely changes performancetool access stops paying off exactly where it should matter most (5). OpenBioRQ is research-assistance benchmark for evaluating literature synthesis and grounding, not clinical decisionsupport tool, and must not be used to guide patient care.

sections.content: answering closed-form medical QA that benchmarks. Biomedical trained genThe is now largely exhausted as eration of models and discriminator. On MedQA (Jin et its kin (Tsatsaronis et al. 2015; Jin et al. 2019; Vilares and Gomez-Rodrıguez 2019; Hendrycks et al. 2021; Pal, Umapathi, frontier systemsfrom the Med-PaLM line (Singhal et al. 2023a; Singhal et al. 2023b) onwardcluster so tightly that recent evaluation finds specialized clinical tools and general LLMs statistically indistinguishable, clustered in an 8-point band at 8896% (Vishwanath et al. 2026). Harder, expert-written sets (GPQA (Rein et al. 2023), LAB-Bench (Laurent et al. 2024)) and even agentic biomedical evaluation (BioMysteryBench (Anthropic 2026)) raise the ceiling, but they raise it within the same regime: every question still has known, verifiable answer. BioMysteryBench makes the assumption explicit, choosing problems whose answers are verifiable by design. What OpenBioRQ inherits from this lineage is its scoring machinery, not its answer model. The closest precedent decomposes free-text answer into reusable rubric statements: long-form MedLFQA/OLAPH grades each response against reference must-have/nice-to-have sentences (Jeong et al. 2024), and HealthBench against physician-written perconversation criteria (Arora et al. 2025). Both still presuppose an answerthe rubric measures how closely response matches curated reference. OpenBioRQs frozen per-question checklist is direct descendant of that decomposition, carried to unsolved questions (JLA priority partnerships, NICE guidance, the open-question literature) that have no consensus answer at construction time; keep the decomposed-rubric machinery but change what it scores from correctness against reference to grounding and acknowledged uncertainty (4). Research-level and open-problem datasets. My construction methodology most directly extends ResearchMath14k (Son et al. 2026): adopt its agentic extract-then-refine pipeline, its LLM-judge self-containment audit (OpenBioRQ attains 85.4% self-contained95% on the retrievalverified track, 75% on expert-consensus; Appendix A), its MiniLM-L6 near-duplicate removal at cosine 0.90, and its separation of behavioral from factuality characterization. then extend it in two ways the open biomedical setting forces. First, openness cannot be taken on faith, so OpenBioRQ is retrieval-grounded: source-framing-only judgment collapsed to zero answered/unknown labels (a confirmation bias), and retrieval-grounded status verifier instead re-confirms each core question against real follow-up evidence (none of the 657 met my resolution criterion). Second, where ResearchMath finds generation paradox of fabricated references, the biomedical agents evaluate barely fabricate at all, so replace its single existence check with two-level audit that continues to content support (5) and exposes real papers that do not support the claim. Tool-using and agentic LLM evaluation. The multiround paradigm adopt was established by ReAct-style reasoningaction interleaving (Yao et al. 2023) and learned tool invocation (Schick et al. 2023), and wave of general agentic benchmarks now scores whether model can select, parameterize, and sequence tools to finish task (Patil et al. 2023; Mialon et al. 2023; Qin et al. 2024; Yao et al. 2024; Trivedi et al. 2024; Wei et al. 2025; Jeong 2026). What unites them is verifiable target: run is correct only if it reaches the known answer or end-state. The same holds in medicine, where AgentClinic (Schmidgall et al. 2024) casts diagnosis as sequential agentic dialogue and MedAgentBench (Jiang et al. 2025) scores tool use against FHIRcompliant EHReach grading against correct diagnosis or completed task. OpenBioRQ removes that target: with no verifiable answer, what measure becomes grounding and abstentionthe behavior actually correct on an unsettled questionwhich ties my setting to selective prediction and calibrated abstention (Kamath, Jia, and Liang 2020; Kadavath et al. 2022). use tools differently from GeneGPT (Jin et al. 2024), which couples an LLM to NCBI endpoints to improve access: the same ten-tool REST stack becomes for me an evaluation instrument, which is what lets me observe agentic collapse (5)a failure mode answer-only benchmarks cannot see. Citation faithfulness, attribution, and grounding. line of work has measured how, and how often, model citations go wrong: that LLMs fabricate plausible-looking references (Walters and Wilder 2023), whether generated statement is attributable to its cited source (Bohnet et al. 2022; Rashkin et al. 2023), and how to make systems cite retrieved evidence inline (Gao et al. 2023). Closest to my L2 question, prior work audits whether the inline citations of generative search engines actually support their statements citation precision and recall, by human raters on generaldomain queries (Liu, Zhang, and Liang 2023). operationalize that question over the citations agents themselves emit during multi-round tool use, rather than curated statement source set, and at scale in biomedicinewhere the picture inverts the math and general-domain findings above: fabrication is near-zero ( 0.7%), while real-but-unsupporting citation is the dominant failure. propose no new attribution metric; the contribution is this empirical inversion (5), with the severity gap posed as hypothesis (Appendix D).

3 The OpenBioRQ Benchmark
One extracted fragment reads only “the pathophysiology of
PSVD remains unclear” (PMID 40578802); OpenBioRQ’s
refine pass turns it into a stand-alone question—“What is
the pathophysiologic mechanism of portosinusoidal vascu-
lar disorder (PSVD)?”—then attaches the three layers that
make an unsolved question gradable. OpenBioRQ is a cor-
pus of such questions, each carrying a retrieval-grounded
openness label (is it actually still open?), an empirical dif-
ficulty signal (do current models fail it?), and a frozen per-
question checklist rubric (how is an answer scored?). The
protocol that consumes those layers is deferred to §4; here

The fix is to re-decide status only from evidence the model actually gathered. retrieval-grounded Stage-2 judge does exactly thisciting evidence IDs, or else falling back to unknownwhich makes resolution detectable at last: it changes the status of 56.5% of 200question sample and reaches unknown for 14%. sections.title:
4 Evaluation Protocol
Consider an open question OpenBioRQ must grade—“Can
therapies targeting the glymphatic system prevent or slow
Alzheimer’s disease?” Its frozen checklist requires an an-
swer to must mention AQP4 polarization as a mech-
anistic target, must acknowledge that no glymphatic-
sections.content: Figure 3: Domain coverage: the 12 top-level taxonomy categories over the OpenBioRQ corpus (12,553 questions); percategory counts are labelled directly on the bars. targeting therapy has entered an AD trial, and must avoid claiming any such therapy is provenso grading reduces to handful of concrete, checkable verdicts. But evaluating genuinely unsolved questions is itself an open problem, for two reasons that compound. First, there is no single correct answer to grade against. Second, the qualities that matter on an open questionevidentiary grounding, calibrated uncertainty, and the avoidance of confident fabricationare precisely the ones brittle stringor embedding-overlap metrics cannot see. OpenBioRQ therefore couples two pieces: an agentic completion harness, in which model actively gathers evidence through biomedical tools, and the per-question frozen checklist just illustrated, which converts open-ended grading into set of concrete, reproducible verdicts (the endto-end flow is in Appendix B, Figure 8).

sections.title:
4.1 Checklist Scoring
The natural first choice—asking a strong LLM to score
each answer along a few abstract dimensions on a contin-
uous scale (Zheng et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2023)—is unre-
liable here, and for a specific reason: with no fixed ref-
erent, different judges (and the same judge across runs)
anchor those dimensions differently, a known hazard of
LLM-as-judge protocols (Zheng et al. 2023) that motivates
structured, rubric-based grading (Liu et al. 2023). The ef-
fect is measurable—replacing free-form dimension scoring
with the frozen checklist below raised inter-judge (LLM-vs-
LLM) agreement from a Spearman correlation of 0.35 to
0.82 (human-expert agreement deferred; see Limitations).
sections.content: The core move is to lift grading from vague dimensions to per-question checklist generated once and then frozen. For each question q, strong model drafts five to eight concrete, checkable, question-specific criteria Cq = {(ti, wi)}iIq , each with type ti and an importance weight wi {1, 2, 3}: must mention key fact, mechanism, or method the answer should include; must acknowledge an uncertainty, gap, or open aspect it must flag (critical for unsolved questions); must ground claim it must back with real cited evidence (PMID, trial, or tool result); must avoid behavior that should not occur (asserting definitive answer to an open question, fabricating citations, or falsely claiming the tools returned nothing). judge reads only the answer text and its tool calls and assigns each criterion verdict vi {1, 0.5, 0} (met/partial/not met; the partial value is the scale midpoint, not tuned parameter). For must avoid the polarity is invertedvi=1 means the prohibited behavior was correctly avoidedso higher score can never reward confabulation. The question score is [0, 1], (1) (cid:80) score(q) = wivi wi bounded and individually auditable. model solves iff scoreT iIq iIq (cid:80) m(q) 0.5 under single deterministic (T =0, one attempt) completionmy headline solve rate. state the decoding temperature wherever it is used, because difficulty turns out to be decoding-sensitive (5.3). The 0.5 cutoff is only reporting convention, not load-bearing threshold: my claims rest on the capability ordering and the cross-tier band, both fixed by the underlying mean scores (0.39/0.44/0.53 for the frontier agents) and therefore robust to where the cutoff is drawn. Freezing the checklist before grading is what drives the agreement gain, because every judge then evaluates the same criteria; it also makes the metric robust to agentic collapse, since collapsed or hedging answer fails its must mention/must ground criteria rather than earning credit for fluent prose. sections.title:
4.2 Agentic Harness and Gold Answers
Each question is answered through multi-round tool use
rather than a single forward pass: a model receives the ques-
tion and access to ten biomedical retrieval tools wrapping
public REST APIs—pubmed, clinicaltrialsgov,
openfda,
uniprot,
pubchem, kegg, ncbi datasets, biomcp—and
may interleave reasoning and tool calls for up to ten rounds
before committing to a final answer. Each trace records the
final answer, the ordered tool calls, token usage, and wall-
clock time, and every question carries a unique task id
of the form source id#k, so multiple questions from one
source paper are scored independently. The wrong-paper
verdict the central result rests on is an LLM-cross-family
proxy; agreement with human medical experts is deferred to
future work, and I release a human-annotation set to enable
it.
sections.content: Each question also comes with an LLM-synthesized gold answer, but use it only as reference context when drafting rubricsnever as target to match or source of ground-truth citations. The reason is empirical, and it is the very hazard the paper studies: 100% of the gold answers cited PMIDs resolve, yet 74% are gold-misattributions (the cited work does not support the claim; LLM-judge, 360, two-judge robust). Treating those citations as ground truth would propagate plausible-looking but unsupported attributions straight into the metric, so instead assess grounding through the must ground criteria, which require the models own answer to cite genuinely supporting evidence (full sub-finding in Appendix D).

Prior work asks the citation question too narrowly. Work on
fabricated references in mathematical and general scientific
writing (Walters and Wilder 2023; Son et al. 2026) treats
a citation as a binary existence problem—does the identi-
fier resolve? That test is necessary, but in biomedicine it is

sections.title:
5.2 Behavioral Analysis of Tool-Using Agents
The agents diverge as sharply in how they engage as in what
they cite. Over the 1,969-question set (Figure 6; full per-
model table in Table 6, Appendix C) I measure the non-
attempt rate, the zero-tool collapse rate (an answer with no
tool call—parametric recitation), average tool calls, and the
cite-rate. Even here the models split: DeepSeek-V4 almost
always attempts (0.8% non-attempt) but collapses to zero
tools most often (31.3%); GLM-5.1 issues the most tool
sections.content: calls (12.6) yet has far higher non-attempt rate (26.2%); and the cite-rate spans 10 (GLM 3.9% vs DeepSeek-V4 38.5%). The split widens on the harder, question-granular priority track, where agentic collapse appears in two of the three roster modelstwo independent lineages. GLM5.1s non-attempt rate jumps to 69% (zero-tool 65%) and DeepSeek-V4s to 62% (zero-tool 62%), each abandoning its tools to decline or recite from memory, while Qwen3.6 stays engaged (zero-tool 22%, flat). That split makes the collapse neither single-model pathology nor an artifact of the questions alonea model-divergent failure that final-answer benchmarks (Son et al. 2026) cannot see (per-model breakdown in Appendix C).

sections.title: 5.3 Agentic Baseline on the Core Set
sections.content: How hard is the core for frontier tool-using agentsthe roster that defined it and held-outs that did not? The buckets were defined at =0.3, where all three roster models scored near 0.3 and solve rate was effectively zero; re-measuring the same core at =0 flips it: the full 657-question core becomes much more solvable (GLM-5.1 26.6%). Empirical difficulty is decoding-sensitive; the 0% was =0.3 artifact. therefore define the frozen core: the 423 of 657 questions all three roster models still fail at =0  (Table 3)  . Hard and discriminating, not an absolute ceiling. Same-lineage open-weight held-outsGLM-5 (Z.ai 2026a) and Qwen3.5-397B (Qwen Team 2026a)solve only 17% (16.6%, 16.8%; overlapping CIs), and an older Qwen3235B (Qwen Team 2025) just 2.1%, so the hardness is not single-roster artifact. Behaviors differ by up to 10; on the harder priority track both GLM-5.1 and DeepSeek-V4 collapse to near-zero tool use while Qwen3.6 stays engaged. which reveal capability range28.8%, 37.8%, 59.6% (ordering and 31-point spread preserved on the churn-stable subset; 5.5). The picture is sharper than 17%: the frozen core is hard at the rosters open-weight tier, yet nonsaturating and discriminatinga strong frontier agent solves majority while even the best leaves 40% unsolved. Because all three independent lineages find it non-trivial, the circularity concern is substantially addressed, though the set is by construction selected on roster failures. Tools confer no measurable advantage. no-tool ablation of GLM-5.1 solves 30.8% [27.3, 34.4] of the full core vs 26.6% [23.4, 30.1] with toolsoverlapping Wilson CIs, so the data do not support that tools help (I anchor to the full core because on the frozen core the roster scores 0% by construction). This is the same-temperature counterpart of the agentic collapse of 5.2: GLM-5.1 is the model most prone to abandoning its tools. The parity replicates on an independent, non-collapsing model: GPT-5.5 solves 59.6% [54.8, 64.1] with tools vs 55.6% [50.8, 60.2] without. It is not rubric artifact: decomposing GLM-5.1s score by criterion type, the no-tool condition is at least as high on every typemust ground included, exactly where retrieval should help (Appendix C). Robust to the checklist judge and across domains. Two pre-specified checks confirm the range is not an artifact (detail in Appendix C): different-family judge (Opus-4.7, 2,498 pairs) shifts each solve rate by 4.1 points while preserving the ordering and the frontier-vs-held-out separation; and across the 12 L1 domains the frozen core spans all twelve, wrong-paper is comparable (818%), and the range holds within the largestonly agentic collapse is domaindependent. sections.title: Medical QA Cannot
sections.content: On MedQA the same six open-weight models sit in saturated 3.9-point band (89.993.8%); on OpenBioRQ they span 7.6 (Figure 7, Table 4). The dissociation is sharp pairwiseDeepSeek-V4 and GLM-5 are within 0.2 MedQA points yet 4 apart on OpenBioRQ, and the top MedQA model (Qwen3.5-397B) is beaten on OpenBioRQ by GLM-5.1. This is resolution gap, not zero shared signal: it is carried by the band and pairwise inversions, not the weak rank correlations (ρ=0.14 core, 0.73 three-exam average; both n=6).

sections.title:
5.5 Reproducibility of the Frozen Core
A second independent T =0 re-decode overturns my own
difficulty headline: membership churns under re-sampling
(46.5% of boundary items and 34.7% of near-threshold
deep-failures flip), so the frozen core is a single-sample
snapshot. I therefore retract an earlier 85.8% retention
estimate and report no single retention figure. The churn
is a property of the selection, not the results: every model
is scored on the identical 423-item set (a fixed released ar-
tifact), the frontier range is unchanged on the churn-stable
subset (24.6/32.7/55.5% on the 346 items stable across
both decodes), and the citation and openness audits are com-
puted elsewhere and untouched. The core is also not idiosyn-
cratic to the three roster models: re-deriving the all-three-fail
set from alternate triples preserves Jaccard 0.70–0.77 under

MedQA has saturated out of discriminating rangea few points of MedQA map to 30-point OpenBioRQ swing. sections.title:
6 Discussion and Limitations
Openness is dated, not eternal. Openness is an as-of
property of OpenBioRQ, not a permanent one: a question
sections.content: open when its source is written may resolve months later as trial reads out. pin it down with retrieval-grounded status verifier rather than parametric beliefa re-audit of the 657 core found none resolved (3)and, because the property is dated, release per-item source records for independent reverification and treat periodic re-auditing as maintenance obligation rather than one-time guarantee. Limitations. The robustness checks lean on (crossfamily judge swaps, per-domain breakdowns, re-decode stability, roster-composition variation) are pre-specified probes of fixed headline claims, not search over hypothesesand report all of them, including the deep-failure test that overturned my own 85.8% retention figure. The L2 wrong-paper rate is judge-relative estimate; only preliminary non-expert human spot-check, not domain-expert validation, exists so far. An independent different-family judge and full-text re-judge both leave the rate intact; the domain-expert κ study is deferred (5; Appendix D). Difficulty is single =0 snapshot and the frozencore selection churns. second =0 decode churns membership, so report no single retention figure, though the range is essentially unchanged on items surviving both decodes (5.5). Difficulty is also modelrelativehard means hard for todays open-weight roster, with the strongest frontier agent still solving majority. Scoring new model still needs live tools. Because rerunning the live selection yields substantially different core, release the fixed 423-item task id list and tool-response replay cache rather than re-runnable procedure; the full release mechanics are in Appendix D. Extended discussion (construction-method transfer, the gold-citation sub-finding, licensing, dual-use, and ethics) is in Appendix D.

sections.title:
7 Conclusion
I presented OpenBioRQ, a retrieval-grounded benchmark
of unsolved biomedical research questions for tool-using
agents—the first agentic biomedical benchmark I am aware
of to target questions with no answer key, scoring ground-
ing and abstention instead of a settled answer. Three proper-
ties make it work, each answering a worry the open setting
raises: openness is verified rather than assumed (a retrieval-
grounded status verifier corrects the confirmation bias of
source-framing refinement; narrative resolution remains un-
detectable by this audit), difficulty is empirical rather than
hand-labeled (a non-saturating frontier range on a frozen
core; §5.3), and evaluation is agentic by construction (multi-
round tool use over ten biomedical APIs graded by frozen
checklist rubrics).
sections.content: My central finding is that existence is not correctness: biomedical agents almost never invent identifiers ( 0.7% over 4,863 citations, the opposite of other domains (Son et al. 2026)), yet roughly 15.9% of their real citations are wrong-paper (10.6% under different-family judge; judge-relative estimate pending expert validation)a resolvable PMID attached to claim it does not support, invisible to an L1-only check. The same setting surfaces agentic collapse: on the hardest questions GLM-5.1 and DeepSeekV4 give zero-tool answers 65%/62% of the time, and notool ablation confers no measurable advantage. The takeaway for biomedical RAG and literature-synthesis systems is concretean existence check is false comfort, so L2 content support, not L1 existence, is the reliability guardrail, caution that extends to LLM-generated reference answers (4). will validate the checklist judge against human experts for calibrated Cohens κ and release the deferred SFT corpus from the 8,931 trajectories already collected. release OpenBioRQ with per-item source records as research-assistance instrument for measuring and improving literature groundingexplicitly not clinical decision support, and no score should be read as fitness to inform patient care. sections.title: Details
sections.content: Construction pipeline. The corpus is built by five-stage pipeline, crawl extract refine dedup export. In brief: authoritative open-problem sources are crawled; an LLM extract agent lifts the distinct open questions each source raises (uncapped, so one paper can yield several, each with unique task id); an LLM refine agent rewrites every question to stand alone and tags it with taxonomy, tool, and difficulty hints (self-containment 51.6% 85.4%); near-duplicate question text is removed (MiniLM-L6 cosine 0.90); and the OpenBioRQ corpus of 12,553 questions over 12 domains is exported. Two labelings are then layered on additively: retrieval-grounded openness (a status verifier feeding Stage-2 judge) and empirical, model-derived difficulty (all-three-fail core frozen core at =0). Crawl. Documents are harvested from authoritative sources that frame open problems rather than report closed results (the per-track sources are detailed in 3.1). Extract. Each crawled document is passed to Claude command-line extractor that lifts the distinct open research questions it raises. Extraction is not capped at one question per document: single source paper can yield several questions. Refine. second Claude pass enriches each extracted question with (i) taxonomy label over 12 level-one categories, (ii) three-axis difficulty hints, (iii) mapping to the relevant medical MCP tools, and (iv) an initial open status with reasoning. The single-pass, tool-less status assigned here is later replaced by retrieval-grounded judgment. Dedup. Near-duplicate questions are collapsed with sentence-embedding screen: questions whose MiniLM-L6 (Wang et al. 2020) embeddings have cosine similarity 0.90 are treated as duplicates. The dedup is over question text, so the multiple distinct questions extracted from one source paper are retained; only genuinely identical questions collapse. Export. The deduplicated questions are exported as the OpenBioRQ corpus, organized into four named tracks expert consensus, retrieval verified, priority setting, sizes and and intended uses are given under Corpus statistics below. expandwhose A.1 Self-Containment Audit question is only useful in isolation if it can be read without its source document; the refine pass is what makes it standalone. On an LLM-judged random sample of the Construction-quality metric Self-containment, overall (n=500) retrieval verified track expert consensus track Self-containment, extractor-only refine pass contribution Dedup threshold (MiniLM-L6 cosine) Value 85.4% 95% 75% 51.6% +33.8 pts 0. Status flip (sourceretrieval, n=200) Still-open audit, core set 56.5% 0/657 resolved Hard contamination, core set (n=657) Hard contamination, expand track (n=483) 0% 0% Table 5: Construction-quality summary: self-containment lift from the refine pass, dedup threshold, grounded statusflip rate, and the still-open / hard-contamination audits. refined output (n = 500), 85.4% of questions are selfcontained, up from 51.6% for the extractor-only baseline +33.8-point lift attributable to the refine pass alone  (Table 5)  . The gain is uneven across tracks: the retrieval verified track comes out substantially cleaner (95%) than expert consensus (75%). A.2 Retrieval-Grounded Openness questions openness is judged from real follow-up evidence, not from models memory of how the source framed the problem. The original source-framing-only refiner produced complete confirmation bias: the corpus contained zero answered or unknown labels. retrievalgrounded Stage-2 judge re-decides openness only from gathered follow-up evidence, making resolution detectable. Stage 1: evidence gathering. non-LLM verifier (status verifier.py) dispatches on the source id type to gather follow-up evidence: PubMed PMIDs are resolved to their citing papers and abstracts via the Europe PMC citation index (Europe PMC 2026); trial NCT identifiers are checked against ClinicalTrials.gov v2 status, completion, and results (U.S. National Library of Medicine 2026); and arXiv identifiers are expanded through Semantic Scholar citations (Allen Institute for AI 2026). (I found NCBI elink citedin unreliable for PMIDs and use Europe PMC as the primary follow-up source.) The bundle of gathered evidence IDs and snippets is attached to each question. Stage 2: grounded re-judgment. Claude judge (stage2 judge.py) re-decides open status only from the gathered evidence and must cite evidence IDs present in the bundle; hallucinated citations are rejected and the item falls back to unknown, so the answered/unknown labels that source-framing never reached become attainable. On random sample of 200 retrieval-verified questions, grounded re-judgment changes the status label of 56.5% of them and assigns unknown to 14% (unreachable under source-framing), confirming that the open label is grounded in evidence rather than an artifact of the sources framing. question-granular priority setting track (n=525), 49% of questions are core, 45% discriminating, and 6% easy (256/236/33 of 525; the bucket composition and per-model mean scores are Figure 2 in the main body). Still-open audit. To confirm that the hardest questions genuinely survive grounded re-judgment, ran still-open audit over the 657 core questions (the trial-completed and no-follow-up flagged subset). Under grounded re-judgment, 0/657 were confirmed resolvedno detectable resolution. Narrative or guideline resolution is undetectable by this audit, and its recall is unquantified. positive control validates the structured-contamination detector: on 34 injected known-contaminated items (real completed-with-results trials relabeled open, and templated source ids) it achieves 100% recall (34/34) and 0/12 false-positives on genuineopen negativesnot blind detector. do not claim the same for the openness verifiers recall against narrative literature resolution (a confirmatory study or guideline that is not trial-status field or template): that is bounded by my conservative, evidence-based criterion rather than positivecontrolled, and may miss not-yet-indexed consolidation. Membership / anti-memorization check. As further membership check, the evaluated question string is the LLM-refined self contained question, not source text. Over the frozen core it shares median longestverbatim span of only 3 words with its source phrasing (median question length 22 words; token Jaccard 0.23), and 86.5% of questions share 5 consecutive verbatim words. So exact-string memorization of benchmark question is largely precluded by constructionthis verbatim-overlap result is the load-bearing membership argument. model-based check is consistent with it: under GLM5.1 the frozen-core questions are higher-perplexity than MedQA-USMLEa long-public benchmark very likely in pretraining corporaby 1.61 nats/token (mean log-prob 3.22 vs 1.61, n=200 each), i.e. the core shows no stronger memorization signature than set already expect to be seen. treat this as corroborating rather than independent: per-token perplexity confounds memorization with question length and syntactic complexity, and the probe uses single roster-lineage model, so it should not be over-read. Both checks target surface memorization (exact strings, perplexity) and would not detect paraphrased exposure of questions underlying problem in pretraining; since my items are LLM-rephrasings of public sources this cannot be excludedthough with no answer key to recall, such exposure confers little advantage, since rote retrieval of settled answer is exactly what an open question denies. A.3 Empirical Difficulty than asDifficulty is read off model behavior signed by hand. Every question is answered with full tool access by the three roster modelsGLM-5.1 (Z.ai 2026b), Qwen3.6 (Qwen Team 2026b), and DeepSeekV4 (DeepSeek-AI 2026)and graded against its frozen checklist rubric. question is labeled core exactly when all three fail it (checklist score < 0.5). On the cleanest, rather Rejected source: preprints. trialed medRxiv (medRxiv 2026) abstracts as an additional source and rejected them. Preprint abstracts overwhelmingly report results rather than frame open questions, so extraction yields almost nothing usable; crawling is instead concentrated on priority-setting and research-gap documents. Question granularity. Because extraction produces several distinct questions per source paper, the unit of the paper. Evevaluation must be the question, not ery question therefore carries unique task id of the form source id#k (e.g., PMID:38345416#0). Keying rubrics, traces, and judgments on source id alone would silently collapse co-extracted questions to sourcepaper granularity and conflate their (often different) difficulties; the #k suffix prevents this collapse, keeping coextracted questions as the distinct items they are. corpus; statistics. OpenBioRQ is form the 12,553-question base Corpus single dataset organized into four named tracks. The retrieval verified (6,648) and expert consensus (5,905) tracks the priority setting track adds 525 questions and the expand track adds 483, each deduplicated against the base. gold-answer-bearing slice of 1,969 questions (mcp benchmark with gold.jsonl) is used for rubric generation and agentic evaluation. Openness is an as-of property: openness labels and difficulty scores reflect their snapshot date and are reproducible against that snapshot. The live literature and tool APIs move, however, so labels gathered at different dates may differ; re-running the affected audits refreshes them. The corpus distribution across the 12 level-one taxonomy categories is shown in the main body (Figure 3). Example questions and checklists. To make the unit of evaluation concrete, show two representative items verbatim, each with its frozen per-question checklist of weighted criteria typed must mention / must acknowledge / must ground / must avoid (4). Example 1 Pharmacology & Drug Discovery, open. Can therapeutic interventions targeting the glymphatic system (the brains perivascular waste clearance pathway) be used to prevent or slow the progression of Alzheimers disease? must mention (w3): sleep enhancement or orexinreceptor antagonists (e.g. suvorexant) as preclinical interventions that enhance clearance. must mention (w2): AQP4 polarization/localization as key mechanistic target. must acknowledge (w3): no therapy specifically targeting the glymphatic system has entered clinical trials for AD. must acknowledge (w3): enhancing glymphatic clearance in humans may not be sufficient to meaningfully alter AD. must ground (w2): cites real preclinical evidence (e.g. Xie et al. 2013, Lundgaard et al.). must avoid (w3): does not claim glymphatictargeting therapies are proven to prevent or slow AD in humans. must avoid (w2): does not fabricate clinical-trial results or citations for human efficacy. Example 2 Medical AI & Informatics, open. How should astrocytes be incorporated into mechanistic, theoretical, and computational models of neural circuits to better understand the pathophysiology of neurological and psychiatric diseases with known astrocytic involvement? must mention (w3): the tripartite-synapse model and calcium-dependent gliotransmitter release. must mention (w2): mean-field or network models incorporating astrocytic glutamate uptake or potassium buffering. must acknowledge (w3): no widely adopted, standardized computational framework currently exists for integrating astrocytes. must acknowledge (w3): the difficulty of defining astrocytic computational primitives (e.g. spatially distributed calcium signals). must ground (w2): cites real established frameworks (e.g. Araque et al., De Pitta et al.). must avoid (w3): does not claim universally accepted astrocyte framework already exists. must avoid (w2): does not fabricate citations or falsely claim tools returned nothing. The must avoid criteria operationalize the wrong-paper and fabrication hazards directly at grading time, and the must acknowledge criteria reward calibrated abstention on what remains unsettled. Definitions, Notation, and Harness This appendix makes the evaluation of 4 precise, one definition at time. Figure 8 sketches the end-to-end flow the definitions below formalize. start from the per-question checklist score and the solve rate it thresholds; build the empirical-difficulty buckets that carve out the core and frozen core; and state the citation and openness predicates behind the wrong-paper and grounded-openness numbers. close with why frozen checklist, rather than free-form judge, is what keeps these definitions stable when an agent collapses. Cq = {(ti, wi)}iIq , each criterion has type tione of must mention, must acknowledge, must ground, must avoidand weight wi {1, 2, 3}, with Iq [5, 8]. judge assigns verdict vi {1, 0.5, 0} via the map µ : {met, partial, not met} {1, 0.5, 0}; the partial value 0.5 is the midpoint of the verdict scale, chosen for interpretability rather than fit. make no robustness claim to its exact value. One type runs the other way. For must avoid criteria the polarity is inverted: vi=1 means the prohibited behaviorfabricating citation, or asserting definitive answer to an open questionwas correctly avoided, so higher score never rewards confabulation. The question score is then score(q) = (cid:0) (cid:80) (cid:1) wi (Eq. 1), and it is well-defined and bounded: because wi 1 and Iq 5 the denominator (cid:80) wi > 0, and since each vi [0, 1] the score lies in [0, 1]a range the must avoid inversion preserves. (cid:1)/(cid:0) (cid:80) wivi iIq iIq iIq Solve rate. The solve rate, my headline metric, is the plainest quantity the score supports: the fraction of fixed question set model solves under single deterministic try. Precisely, model solves iff scoreT m(q) τ with τ =0.5 (the superscript records the decoding temperature), and the headline number fixes =0deterministic, one attempt. avoid the label pass@k on purpose: there is sample count, whereas my 0.5 is score threshold over fixed denominator. Symmetrically, fails iff scoreT m(q) < 0.5, and the difficulty threshold used throughout the main text is exactly this τ =0.5 instance. Empirical difficulty, core, and frozen core. Difficulty here is not assigned by hand; it is read off the roster. Fix the roster of modelsM =3, namely {GLM-5.1, Qwen3.6, DeepSeek-V4}and let FT (q) = {m : scoreT m(q) < 0.5} count how many of them fail at temperature . The empirical-difficulty bucket is just that count: is all3 fail iff F0.3(q) = , all3 pass iff F0.3(q) = 0, and split otherwise. The core keeps only the all-fail items that also survive the audits, as below, = {q : F0.3(q) = resolved(q)=0 hardContam(q)=0}, i.e. the all-roster-fail itemsat the temperature =0.3 at which the buckets were originally definedthat additionally pass the openness and contamination checks, where resolved(q), hardContam(q) {0, 1} are the indicator outcomes of the audits of 3.2 (detailed in Appendix A). The frozen core then tightens this one step further, keeping only the items that stay all-roster-fail under deterministic decoding, = {q : F0(q) = } C, which removes items that only looked hard under stochastic decodingso 423 657 by construction. Checklist score. The score of an answer is how much of its frozen checklist it satisfies, weighted by each criterions importance. In detail: for question with frozen checklist Citation audit and openness predicates. Two predicates turn raw audit outcomes into the numbers report. Take Figure 8: The OpenBioRQ evaluation flow (companion to the construction pipeline in Appendix A). One open questionwith no answer key, plus its frozen per-question checklistis answered by an agentic harness that interleaves reasoning and tool calls (up to ten rounds) over ten biomedical REST APIs. The resulting trace (final answer, cited PMIDs, tool calls) is graded by two independent measures: the frozen checklist (weighted; 0.5 = solve) yields the solve rate, and the two-level citation audit (L1 existence, L2 support) yields the wrong-paper ratethe apparatus behind my difficulty and citation findings, respectively. citations first. Each emitted identifier carries fetch outcome fetch {found, notfound, transient}, and L1 existence counts notfound as fabrication while excluding transient. For found citation, an L2 support label supp {yes, partial, no} asks whether the cited record actually substantiates the claim; the wrong-paper case is the narrow one, found supp=no, and compute wrongpaper rates over found citations only. Openness is the second predicate. The Stage-2 status S(q) takes values in {open, partially answered, answered, unknown} and is tied to the gathered evidence set E(q): every identifier it cites must lie in that set, cites(S(q)) E(q) (3.2). Agreement is always measured between judge pairsSpearman over per-answer checklist scores for the LLM-vs-LLM correlation, and Cohens κ over the wrong-paper categories. Rubric generation. The rubric generator is held to one rule: every criterion must be binary-checkable and instancegrounded. Identifies AQP4 depolarization as candidate mechanism is admissible; Discusses the mechanism well is not. It must also emit at least one must acknowledge and one must avoid criterion per question, so that calibrated uncertainty and the absence of fabrication are always scored rather than left to the judges discretion. Robustness to agentic collapse. practical advantage of checklist scoring is its robustness to agentic collapse. On the hardest questions, models frequently degrade into nonattempts or zero-tool traces. continuous free-form judge tends to award such empty or hedging answers partial credit for fluent proseblurring the very distinction the benchmark is designed to expose. The frozen checklist instead withholds credit precisely where it matters: collapsed answer fails its must mention and must ground criteria and, if it falsely claims the tools yielded nothing, also fails must avoid, so the score reflects the absence of grounded substance rather than surface fluency. This keeps the difficulty buckets stable and makes the resulting core set reliable target for measuring progress. Extended Experiments and Robustness Each headline in 5 is pooled number; here open it up. begin with the citation audit at full per-model resolution and its robustness probes; the behavioral breakdown; the notool ablation by criterion; two robustness checks (checklist judge, domains); and the reproducibility accounting behind the frozen core. C.1 Citation Audit: Robustness Probes The full per-model L1/L2 breakdown is Figure 5 in the main body; here report the robustness probes behind the headline wrong-paper rate. Conservative floor: the partial bin. Wrong-paper excludes the large partial bin (28.1% of evaluable citations under the Opus judge). strict binary re-judge of substantive partials (tangential, wrong-population, or wrongdirection support counted as not-supporting) flips sizable fraction to no (in 25-item probe, 10/25 = 40% flip), so folding partials in would push the upper bound to 25%; the no-only definition under-reports rather than inflates the failure. Wrong-paper by identifier type. Splitting by identifier type, trial-registry citations are misused more often than publication citations: under the Opus judge, NCT trial IDs are wrong-paper 20.3% (47/231) versus 13.0% for PMIDs (593/4,545)mirroring their higher fabrication at L1 (GLM-5.1s invented identifiers were predominantly NCT IDs). Model Nonattempt Zero-tool collapse Avg tools GLM-5.1 Qwen3.6 DeepSeek-V 26.2% 19.5% 0.8% 20.8% 12.6 6.1 19.6% 8.3 31.3% Citerate 3.9% 23.9% 38.5% Table 6: Behavioral analysis on the 1,969-question set. Models diverge sharply on every axis. Cite-rate spans roughly 10 (GLM 3.9% vs. DeepSeek-V4 38.5%). Wrong-paper is sourcing defect, not marker of ungrounded reasoning. Across 879 jointly-labeled answers (those with audited citations), wrong-paper citation shows no detectable association with whether the answer satisfies the must ground criterion: risk ratio 1.07 (95% CI 0.88 1.31). The decoupling is not an artifact of lenient grounding check. Of the must ground-passing answers that carry wrong-paper citation, 73% also carry genuinely supporting one (every answer cites 2 sources); and under strict grounding definition (requiring an actually-supporting citation) the association is still null (RR 1.06, 95% CI 0.99 1.13; odds-ratio point estimate 1.33 [0.93, 1.89], so independence is not refuted rather than positively established). The join is at answer rather than per-claim granularity. The takeaway: wrong-paper is sourcing-integrity defect that persists even in answers that otherwise look well-grounded, so neither an existence check nor grounding-reward signal would surface it. C.2 Behavioral Divergence The starkest gap is the cite-rate: it spans roughly 10 across the rosterGLM 3.9% versus DeepSeek-V4 38.5%so the three models barely agree on whether to ground at alland that is only one axis; they diverge as sharply on the others. The full per-model breakdown over the 1,969-question set is in Table 6 (the figure is in the main body, Figure 6). Agentic collapse is concentrated on the hardest track and multi-model. On the broad 1,969-set the zero-tool rate is only moderate for all three roster models (Table 6: 20.8/19.6/31.3%). Collapse is specific to the harder, question-granular priority track, where two of the three jump sharply: GLM-5.1 to 65.3% zero-tool (non-attempt 69.1%, up from 26.2%) and DeepSeek-V4 to 62.3% (non-attempt 62.3%, up from 0.8%), while Qwen3.6 barely moves (22.1% zero-tool, non-attempt 26.3%). Because collapse spans two independent developers (Zhipu and DeepSeek) it is not single-model idiosyncrasy; because the third (Alibabas Qwen3.6) resists, it is not forced by the questions alone. It is model-divergent, non-monotone failure, not uniform lawa structural axis of open-question behavior that answer-only benchmarks cannot register. C.3 No-Tool Ablation: Criterion Decomposition The tool-parity result is not rubric artifact. Decomposing GLM-5.1s score by criterion type over the 656 matched core questions, the no-tool condition scores at least as high on every type, and its largest gains are on must mention (factual recall, 32.5 40.2) and must avoid (77.7 84.7), not on the abstention-credit must acknowledge (5.5 7.5, the smallest gain). Even on must ground which rewards citing real supporting evidence, exactly where retrieval should helptools confer no advantage (17.1 with tools vs 19.3 without). C.4 Leaderboard Robustness to the Checklist

sections.title: Judge
sections.content: Because the leaderboard is graded by GLM-5.1 checklist judge (same lineage as roster model), re-grade the frozencore solve rate of all six non-roster models with an independent different-family judge (Opus-4.7, 2,498 questionmodel pairs). Per-model solve rate shifts by at most 4.1 points (Gemini-3-Pro 28.8 27.8, Opus-4.7 37.8 40.5, GPT-5.5 59.6 58.4, held-out GLM-5 16.6 20.7); the per-question solve label agrees at Cohens κ = 0.65, and both the frontier ordering (Gemini < Opus < GPT-5.5) and the frontier-vs-held-out separation (min frontier 27.8% > max held-out 20.7%) survive intact (the Opus rates are over the successfully-parsed subset of the 423 per model). The capability range is therefore not an artifact of the GLMlineage judge. C.5 Per-Domain Breakdown Because every headline is pooled number, re-aggregate each by the L1 taxonomy  (Table 7)  and ask which findings survive the split. Three hold up; one does not. Take wrongpaper first: its Opus rate runs from 8.1% in Surgical to 18.1% in Genomicsa narrow band in which no single specialty drives the pooled rate, so the defect is not ClinicalMedicine artifact. The frozen core is similarly spread: it spans all twelve domains, the largest share being Clinical Medicine at 21.5%, so it is not single-specialty benchmark wearing general label. The frontier capability range survives the split too, and holds within domains: in each of the four largest the Gemini < Opus < GPT-5.5 ordering is preserved and GPT-5.5 solves majorityClinical Medicine 24 38 62, Neuroscience 23 37 62, Oncology 27 38 60, Infectious Disease 38 48 50with only minor inversions in low-n domains. The one finding that does not survive uniformly is agentic collapse: GLM-5.1s zerotool rate peaks in Medical AI & Informatics (43.9%) and Surgical Sciences (29.0%) but falls to 10.5% in Oncology, suggesting collapse is triggered more where the biomedical APIs return sparse hits. C.6 Reproducibility of the Frozen Core: Full

sections.content: I re-decode all three roster models second time at =0, five days later (testing live-API and agentic nondeterminism; the model output itself is deterministic), and re-judge on the same rubric. Per-question scores reproduce closely (mean absolute checklist-score delta 0.090.14; fail-label agreement 0.780.81). Membership, however, churns: of the boundary-proximal items 46.5% flip, and direct test of the near-threshold deep failures (max-of-3 in [0.35, 0.40)) rc wp frontier solve rate coll. The frontier range (Gem < Opus < GPT) holds in every domain with 18, and wrong-paper is broadly comparable across domains. < 10 frozen-core itemsnot interpretable. finds 34.7% (17/49) also flip to passdriven by live-API drift and erratic agentic tool use (Qwen3.6 and DeepSeekV4 account for 19 of 23 flips). therefore retract an earlier 85.8% retention estimate (which had assumed deepfailures fixed) and report no single retention figure; the frozen core is single-T =0 snapshot, not seed-stable partition. The churn is property of the selection, not the comparisons. Recomputing the frontier range on the 346 items stable across both decodes gives 24.6/32.7/55.5% (vs the full-423 28.8/37.8/59.6%)same ordering and 31-point spreadand the 77 flipped items are simply easier for the frontier agents (48.1/61.0/77.9%). The core is also not idiosyncratic to the three roster models: re-deriving the allthree-fail set from alternate triples preserves Jaccard 0.70 0.77 under single-model substitution and recovers 72% (Jaccard 0.58) under full same-lineage roster swap, so the core is largely property of the open-weight capability tier. test only removals; whether re-decode adds items with different frontier profile would require re-decoding the noncore questions, which leave to future work. Extended Discussion, Release, and Ethics Why citation severity is medical, not math, problem. My two-level audit is motivated by severity asymmetry hypothesize is specific to medicine. In domain like mathematics, fabricated reference is academic sloppiness (Walters and Wilder 2023; Son et al. 2026); in medicine, argue, real identifier (a resolvable PMID or NCT number) attached to an unsupported clinical claim is plausibly more consequential misattribution, because the resolving identifier can lend false authority and downstream reader may be more likely to trust the claim because the link works. (This is sourcing and attribution argument, not clinical-harm measurement: do not study patient impact, claim veracity, or reader trust.) This is why L1-existence alone is the wrong instrument: emitted identifiers almost always exist ( 0.7% non-existent over 4,863 citations, the opposite of ResearchMaths 54% fabrication rate), so the meaningful failure mode lives at L2real papers cited for claims they do not support (15.9% of real citations overall, judgerelative LLM estimatesee the non-expert spot-check below). Existence is not correctness, and in biomedicine the gap between them is the reliability story. Open questions as faithfulness-and-abstention probe. Hand model an open question and it has two basic moves: abstainhonestly flag that the literature does not settle the matteror confabulateassert confident answer, often pinned to an unsupporting citation. Both of my headline findings are failures of this choice. Agentic collapse is abstention taken to its degenerate extreme: the agent stops engaging altogether rather than commit. Wrong-paper citation is the opposite failure, confabulation dressed in resolvable source link: definitive answer made to look sourced. Closed-form QA can expose neither, because the answer is already given; open questions are what make the abstain-vsconfabulate trade-off the object of measurement. Empirical, model-derived difficulty as transferable construction method. The difficulty-labeling recipe answer every candidate with roster of models and bucket by all-model failure (solve rate)is contribution independent of the biomedical content, and it earns that independence through three properties. It needs no hand-labeling: difficulty is read off the rosters answers rather than assigned by an annotator, so human never has to judge what is hard. It scales with corpus size: adding candidates costs only more model passes, not more human effort, so the labeled pool grows as cheaply as the raw pool. And it is intrinsically nonsaturating: as models improve, items that were core migrate to discriminating and fresh core can be redefined against the current frontier, so the benchmark never permanently bottoms out the way fixed human-labeled set does. The method is domain-agnosticit should transfer to any setting where frozen, checkable rubric can be generated, reusable way to build hard benchmarks without human difficulty annotation. My own two corrections carry the generalizable lessons for anyone using this recipe in an agentic setting. First, empirical difficulty measured under stochastic decoding can be partly sampling artifact: my solve rate 0% core dissolved to 1 in 4 solvable once re-measured at =0so pipelines that bucket by model failure must fix and report the decoding temperature. Second, even at =0 the signal is not reproducible if the environment is live, because run-to-run tool drift and erratic agentic behavior churn membership (34.7% of even my near-threshold deep failures flip on re-decode, 5.5). The recipe for agentic difficulty is therefore not just bucket by all-model failure but bucket by all-model failure over frozen tool environmentone must record and replay the tool responses (a difficulty signal taken over moving environment is not reproducible, whatever the temperature). The frozen replay cache release (Data availability) is the concrete instantiation. standalone insight: synthesized gold answers are citation-unreliable. Separately from the model-trajectory audit, surface finding of independent interest: LLMsynthesized gold answers cite 100% real PMIDs but point to the wrong paper 74% of the time (the full LLM-judge audit, 360; 4). This is not single-judge artifact: an independent different-family judge (Opus-4.7) re-judging stratified sample puts the wrong-paper rate at 72.8%, against the primary judges 73.5% on the same keys (n=147). (The two judges agree at the item level as well, but do not lean on the inter-judge κ here: with 73% base rate it is inflated and uninformative; the load-bearing fact is simply that both families report 73%.) The claim still rests on title+abstract rather than full text and is not humanadjudicated, but it carries the same two-judge floor as my trajectory result. The finding cuts two ways. For my internal use it is conservative: if gold citations are even partly unreliable, grounding against them is unsafe, which is why grade against rubrics instead. As standalone cautionary result for the now-common practice of using LLM-generated reference answers as ground truthit is robust to the choice of judge. keep this audit strictly separate from the trajectorycitation audit and never use gold citations as target. On the LLM-in-the-loop construction and subjectjudge overlap. Opus-4.7 re-judges the entire L2 citation population (10.6% vs the primary 15.9%; binary κ=0.755), re-grades the full leaderboard (every solve rate within 4.1 points, range and frontier-vs-held-out separation preserved, κ=0.65), and re-judges the gold-citation audit (72.8% vs 73.5% on the same keys).
